title: Turn a topic into a module outline
teaser: Skip the blank page. Hand AI a topic, get back a sequenced, outcome-driven module outline you can actually use.
body:
You've got a topic and a deadline. Maybe some bullet points from a kickoff call. The blank document is staring at you. This is the place to use AI as a starting-point engine — not for the final product, but for the structure you'll build on.

What makes an outline actually useful isn't a list of topics. It's a sequence that takes a learner from where they are to where they need to be, with each module building on the last. The prompt below bakes in a few instructional design moves so what you get back isn't generic:

  • Each module needs a stated outcome (not just a topic).
  • Each module needs a one-line rationale — so you know why it sits in that position.
  • The module count is capped — fights the AI urge to over-deliver.

Optional upgrade: if you have actual source material — SOPs, transcripts, articles — drop it all into a NotebookLM notebook and run the prompt in there. The outline comes back grounded in your sources, with citations. Way less hallucination, much easier to defend to an SME.

What it won't do: Validate that the outline is right for your audience. AI doesn't know your learners. After you have an outline, you still need to check it against what you learned in Analysis & Discovery.

This prompt uses the FAST model — Frame · Ask · Shape · Tune. Every Prompt card in the playbook follows it.
steps: Have your topic, audience description, and any source bullet points ready. The more you give it up front, the better the first draft.
Paste the prompt into Claude or ChatGPT (or into a NotebookLM notebook if you've loaded sources). Fill in the brackets.
Iterate. Ask for a different sequence, a different module count, a different cognitive progression. The first output is rarely the final outline.

prompt_fast.frame: You are a senior instructional designer.
prompt_fast.ask: Draft a module-by-module outline for a training program.
prompt_fast.shape:
Topic: [TOPIC]
Audience: [WHO THE LEARNERS ARE — role, expertise level, motivation]
Format: [e.g., 60-minute live workshop / 4-hour async / 30-min microlearning]
Source material (optional): [BULLET POINTS OR PASTED SOURCE CONTENT]

Rules:
- 4 to 6 modules. Don't go over.
- Each module needs: a title, a stated outcome (use a Bloom-level verb), 3–5 sub-points, and a one-line rationale for why this module sits in this position.
- Sequence the modules so each one enables the next.
prompt_fast.tune: Where you assumed something, call it out — those assumptions become the questions I bring to the SME. If the brief is too thin to design from, ask me one clarifying question first instead of guessing.

pro_tip: Try backward design. Describe what the learner needs to do at the end — what they'd be doing in the final assessment or on the job — and ask AI to outline backwards from there. The result is always sharper than "outline this topic." Wiggins & McTighe wrote a whole book about why, but you can feel the difference on your next outline.
